card.indications: Induction and maintenance of general anesthesia, procedural sedation, and analgesia in monitored settings. Intranasal esketamine products are separate formulations used for selected depressive disorders under restricted programs.
card.mechanism: Produces dissociative anesthesia primarily through noncompetitive blockade of NMDA receptors, reducing excitatory glutamate transmission. It also has analgesic and sympathomimetic effects.
card.dosing: Dose is highly indication-, route-, age-, and setting-specific. IV induction commonly uses about 1 to 2 mg/kg; IM induction commonly uses about 4 to 10 mg/kg. Lower IV doses may be used for analgesia or procedural sedation. Titrate only with continuous monitoring and trained personnel.
card.routeAdministration: Administer only where airway equipment, oxygen, suction, and resuscitation medications are immediately available. Give IV doses slowly to reduce respiratory depression and hemodynamic instability. Monitor continuously during and after administration until protective reflexes and mental status recover.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity and conditions in which a significant rise in blood pressure would create serious risk. Use caution with severe cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, elevated intracranial or intraocular pressure, psychosis, substance-use disorder, and increased aspiration risk.
card.warnings: Can cause respiratory depression or apnea, laryngospasm, emergence reactions, hypertension, tachycardia, increased secretions, and abuse or dependence. Repeated or prolonged exposure has been associated with urinary and hepatobiliary toxicity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, nausea, vomiting, increased blood pressure or pulse, nystagmus, diplopia, hypersalivation, vivid dreams, confusion, and agitation during recovery.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Apnea, laryngospasm, severe hypertension, arrhythmia, aspiration, anaphylaxis, severe emergence delirium, urinary-tract injury with chronic misuse, and hepatotoxicity.
card.interactions: Opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, sedating antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory risk. Sympathomimetics and vasopressin may increase blood pressure. Theophylline may lower the seizure threshold.
card.monitoring: Continuously monitor airway patency, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, end-tidal carbon dioxide when available, blood pressure, pulse, ECG, level of consciousness, secretions, and recovery from sedation. Assess for misuse with repeated therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Explain that vivid dreams, confusion, or unusual sensations may occur during recovery. Do not drive, drink alcohol, make important decisions, or stay alone until cleared. Report painful urination or persistent abdominal symptoms with repeated treatment.
card.nutrition: Follow fasting instructions before anesthesia or procedural sedation. Resume fluids and food only after swallowing, alertness, and nausea are assessed. Avoid alcohol and other sedatives.

card.indications: Short-term management of moderately severe acute pain that requires analgesia at the opioid level. Oral tablets are continuation therapy after IV or IM dosing. Ophthalmic and nasal products have formulation-specific indications.
card.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby decreasing pain, inflammation, and fever while also impairing platelet function.
card.dosing: For systemic therapy, the combined duration of IV, IM, and oral ketorolac must not exceed 5 days in adults. Oral continuation is commonly 10 mg every 4 to 6 hours as needed, maximum 40 mg/day. Parenteral dosing is lower in older adults, low-weight patients, or renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Verify total days of therapy and all prior ketorolac doses before administration. Give oral doses with food or milk if needed. Ensure adequate hydration. Do not combine with aspirin or another NSAID. Follow exact product directions for eye drops or nasal spray.
card.contraindications: Active peptic ulcer disease or GI bleeding, advanced renal impairment or volume depletion, high bleeding risk, aspirin- or NSAID-related asthma or allergy, labor and delivery, perioperative CABG pain, concurrent NSAID use, and use as prophylactic analgesia before major surgery.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include serious or fatal GI bleeding, ulceration, perforation, cardiovascular thrombotic events, renal injury, bleeding, and hypersensitivity. Use beyond 5 days increases toxicity without improving benefit.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, dyspepsia, abdominal pain, headache, dizziness, drowsiness, edema, injection-site pain, and local eye or nasal irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: GI hemorrhage or perforation, acute kidney injury, myocardial infarction, stroke, severe hypertension, anaphylaxis, bronchospasm, major surgical bleeding, severe skin reactions, and hepatotoxicity.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, SSRIs, SNRIs, corticosteroids, alcohol, and other NSAIDs increase bleeding. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics increase renal risk. Lithium and methotrexate concentrations may rise.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain response, total treatment duration, renal function, blood pressure, hydration, urine output, CBC when indicated, and signs of GI bleeding, edema, allergy, or cardiovascular events.
card.patientTeaching: Use only for the prescribed short course and do not add ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin, or other NSAIDs. Report black stools, vomiting blood, reduced urine, chest pain, shortness of breath, facial swelling, or severe rash immediately.
card.nutrition: Take oral doses with food or milk if stomach upset occurs and maintain hydration unless restricted. Avoid alcohol because it increases GI bleeding risk.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension. IV labetalol is used for severe hypertension in monitored settings, including selected hypertensive emergencies and pregnancy-related severe hypertension.
card.mechanism: Blocks beta1, beta2, and alpha1 receptors, lowering heart rate, myocardial contractility, renin release, and peripheral vascular resistance.
card.dosing: Oral therapy commonly begins at 100 mg twice daily and is titrated to response. IV regimens may use repeated boluses or a continuous infusion according to protocol. Older adults and patients with hepatic impairment generally require conservative dosing.
card.routeAdministration: Check blood pressure and apical pulse before administration and follow hold parameters. Give with food consistently to reduce variability in absorption. Administer IV doses slowly with continuous blood-pressure and cardiac monitoring; keep the patient supine during and shortly after IV therapy.
card.contraindications: Bronchial asthma or obstructive airway disease, overt heart failure, greater-than-first-degree heart block, cardiogenic shock, severe bradycardia, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause severe orthostatic hypotension, bradycardia, heart block, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm, and hepatic injury. Abrupt withdrawal may precipitate angina, myocardial infarction, or severe hypertension.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, fatigue, nausea, scalp tingling, orthostatic hypotension, bradycardia, nasal congestion, and sexual dysfunction.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypotension, syncope, heart block, cardiogenic shock, bronchospasm, hepatic failure, anaphylaxis, and masking of hypoglycemia.
card.interactions: Other antihypertensives, nitrates, anesthetics, and alcohol increase hypotension. Digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, amiodarone, and other rate-slowing drugs increase bradycardia or heart block. Beta agonists may be less effective.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, ECG with IV therapy, orthostatic symptoms, respiratory status, heart-failure findings, liver symptoms, and glucose awareness in patients with diabetes.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food in the same manner each day and do not stop suddenly. Rise slowly. Report fainting, wheezing, swelling, a very slow pulse, chest pain, dark urine, or jaundice.
card.nutrition: Take consistently with food. Follow prescribed sodium guidance and avoid excessive alcohol because it can worsen hypotension.

card.indications: Treatment of constipation and prevention or treatment of portal-systemic encephalopathy by reducing intestinal ammonia absorption.
card.mechanism: Colonic bacteria metabolize lactulose into organic acids that draw water into the bowel, soften stool, and acidify the colon. Acidification converts absorbable ammonia to ammonium and promotes its excretion.
card.dosing: Constipation dosing is individualized to produce a soft bowel movement, commonly 15 to 30 mL daily. Hepatic-encephalopathy regimens use larger or more frequent doses titrated to about 2 to 3 soft stools daily. Rectal administration may be used when oral therapy is not possible.
card.routeAdministration: Measure liquid accurately. It may be mixed with water, juice, or milk to improve taste. For hepatic encephalopathy, titrate to the prescribed stool target rather than diarrhea. Follow institutional technique for retention enemas.
card.contraindications: Patients requiring a low-galactose diet and hypersensitivity. Use caution with diabetes, electrolyte imbalance, bowel obstruction symptoms, and prolonged high-dose therapy.
card.warnings: Excess dosing can cause severe diarrhea, dehydration, hypernatremia, hypokalemia, and other electrolyte abnormalities. Abdominal pain or suspected obstruction requires evaluation before use.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Gas, bloating, belching, abdominal cramping, nausea, and diarrhea.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe dehydration, hypernatremia, hypokalemia, aspiration in patients with impaired swallowing, and clinically important acid-base or electrolyte disturbances.
card.interactions: Other laxatives may cause excessive diarrhea. Drugs that alter colonic pH or intestinal flora may change the effect in hepatic encephalopathy. Monitor medications affected by potassium loss or dehydration.
card.monitoring: Monitor bowel frequency and consistency, mental status and ammonia-related symptoms, hydration, abdominal distention, sodium, potassium, and other electrolytes during high-dose or prolonged therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Measure each dose, expect gas initially, and drink fluids unless restricted. For liver disease, aim for the prescribed number of soft stools and report persistent diarrhea, weakness, confusion, or reduced urine.
card.nutrition: May be mixed with water, milk, or fruit juice. Maintain adequate fluids unless restricted. Patients with diabetes should account for product-specific sugar content.

card.indications: Treatment of focal seizures, primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. Also used for maintenance treatment of bipolar I disorder.
card.mechanism: Inhibits voltage-sensitive sodium channels and reduces release of excitatory neurotransmitters, including glutamate.
card.dosing: Must be started low and increased slowly. The schedule depends heavily on age, indication, valproate use, and enzyme-inducing drugs. Valproate requires much lower doses; carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone, and rifampin may require higher doses. Restarting after an interruption may require repeating the initial titration.
card.routeAdministration: Give consistently with or without food. Swallow extended-release tablets whole. Chewable or dispersible tablets may be chewed, swallowed whole, or dispersed according to product directions. Verify the titration schedule carefully.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to lamotrigine or product components.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: serious and sometimes fatal rashes, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis. Risk increases with high starting doses, rapid titration, valproate, and younger age. Other risks include aseptic meningitis, blood dyscrasias, suicidal thoughts, and multiorgan hypersensitivity.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, headache, diplopia, blurred vision, ataxia, nausea, vomiting, insomnia, somnolence, and rash.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, DRESS or multiorgan hypersensitivity, hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis, aseptic meningitis, severe blood dyscrasias, suicidal behavior, and status epilepticus after abrupt withdrawal.
card.interactions: Valproate markedly increases lamotrigine concentrations and rash risk. Enzyme-inducing antiseizure drugs and rifampin lower concentrations. Estrogen-containing contraceptives may lower lamotrigine levels, and levels can rise during the hormone-free interval.
card.monitoring: Monitor seizure control or mood stability, rash and mucosal lesions, fever, lymph nodes, liver symptoms, CBC and liver tests when indicated, coordination, vision, mood, suicidal thinking, and adherence to titration.
card.patientTeaching: Stop the medication and seek immediate guidance for any new rash, blistering, mouth sores, fever, or facial swelling. Do not restart after a rash without prescriber approval. Do not stop abruptly and report mood changes or suicidal thoughts.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain consistent use and discuss major changes in hormonal contraceptives. Avoid alcohol if it worsens dizziness or sedation.
